title: Rolex Datejust 16234 Tropical Dial
description: The Rolex Datejust was introduced in 1945 to mark the company’s 40th anniversary. It was the first self-winding wristwatch to feature an automatically changing date window at 3 o’clock, setting a new standard for how the date was displayed on a wristwatch. Since then, the Datejust has become one of the defining pillars of the Rolex collection—an enduring classic that helped shape the modern date watch as we know it today. At hand we have a Rolex Datejust 16234, elevated by the fluted white gold bezel that adds just the right touch of contrast to the steel case and bracelet. This example really stands out thanks to its dial. The original silver dial has developed a tropical transformation, now taking on a unique “orange to creme brûlée” hue. The result is striking, giving the watch a completely individual character with a warm, almost creamy tone that makes it one of a kind. The 36mm case is in great condition, and the fluted bezel remains sharp and well defined. It is fitted with a solid-link Oyster bracelet (ref. 78360) with end links stamped 558B and a clasp marked DE9. The quickset movement has been fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker and is ready for wear. That “Tropical” dial hits different, drop us a message! title: Rolex French Case Calatrava Dress Watch Gold Dial in 18k Rose Gold
description: Everybody has heard of the Rolex Daytona, Submariner and Day-Date — the models that helped shape the brand into what it is today. Yet hidden beneath those icons are smaller, lesser-known chapters of Rolex history that remain largely undiscovered outside dedicated collecting circles. Presented here is exactly that: a rare and elegant 18k rose gold “Calatrava”-style Rolex dress watch made for the French market. So-called “French case” Rolex watches represent a fascinating historical anomaly. Produced primarily during and shortly after the Second World War, these watches featured locally manufactured gold cases made in France rather than Switzerland. The reason was largely economic rather than stylistic. During the post-war years, the French government imposed strict import restrictions on precious metals, including Swiss gold, in an effort to protect the domestic economy. To navigate these regulations, Rolex supplied movements and dials to selected French retailers and casemakers, who then produced the cases locally before completing the watches for sale within France. These French-made cases are highly prized by collectors today, not only because of their rarity, but also due to their distinctive aesthetic. Freed from the more conservative Swiss case designs of the period, French casemakers often introduced elegant Calatrava-inspired profiles and subtle Art Deco influences that give these watches a unique personality entirely of their own. This particular example features a beautifully proportioned 34mm rose gold case with a thin smooth bezel that visually enlarges the dial, allowing the watch to wear larger on the wrist than its dimensions might suggest. The case also retains its solid end bars — a lovely period detail. The rose gold dial is exceptionally refined, featuring a delicate minute track with alternating black Roman numerals for the even hours, Arabic numerals and fine baton markers creating a beautifully balanced and distinctly European aesthetic. The dial remains free from lume and carries a simple “Swiss Made” designation at 6 o’clock. Completing the elegant appearance are slender stick hands with blued hour and minute hands, adding just the right amount of subtle contrast against the warm rose gold tones of the dial and case. This rare and charming dress Rolex has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is fitted with a lovely Bulang & Sons leather strap. Calatrava proportions, Rolex reliability, unmistakable character , Far removed from sports Rolex — and all the more charming for it ! Specifications: Rolex Dresswatch in 18 k rose gold 34mm case with solid end-bars, snap back case-back Serial 75xx 24xx, 70xx dating to the 1940’s No-lume Rose gold dial Stick style no lume hands Pictured Bulang and sons leather strap Fully serviced by our watchmaker One-year mechanical warranty Free worldwide Fedex Priority shipping

title: Heuer Camaro ref 7743 N Tachymeter Tropical Brown Dial
description: The Heuer Camaro was introduced in the late 1960s under the leadership of Jack Heuer. True to the brand’s strong motorsport focus at the time, the name “Camaro” was borrowed from the Chevrolet Camaro—a deliberate move to strengthen Heuer’s appeal in the American market. The timing made sense. The Camaro had just built a strong reputation, notably serving as the pace car for both the Indianapolis 500 and the American Road Race of Champions at Daytona International Speedway in 1967—firmly embedding the name in the racing world. At 37mm, the Camaro is slightly larger than the Carrera’s 36mm, but on the wrist the two share a very similar feel. The dial layout and handset follow the same clean, functional approach seen across Carreras of the period—making the Camaro a slightly more playful take on a familiar formula. At hand we have a reference 7743N. The case is in lovely condition, showing a strong sunray finish, paired with pump pushers and a screw-down case back—exactly the details you want to see. The dial really makes this one stand out: a rich milk-chocolate brown tone, contrasted by two glossy black subdials for the chronograph functions and a crisp white tachymeter scale—perfect for timing speed, whether on the track or just in theory. It’s paired with a racing-style Bulang & Sons leather strap and fitted with a steel Heuer-signed buckle—finishing the look just right. Inside, the hand-wound Valjoux 7730 movement has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready to go. A proper vintage racer—get in touch! title: Rolex Oversized Oyster-Perpetual 1012 Silver Swiss Only Dial in 18k Yellow Gold - 36 mm
description: The Rolex reference 1012 is a very rare and oversized 36mm Oyster Perpetual dress watch, produced primarily during the late 1950s and early 1960s. Manufactured exclusively in solid gold — either yellow or rose — it offered a more restrained and elegant alternative to the Datejust, omitting the date function in favour of a cleaner and more symmetrical dial layout. The reference 1012 features a solid 18k gold 36mm Oyster case with a smooth bezel, giving the watch a particularly refined and understated appearance. It is closely related to the reference 1013, which shared the same no-date configuration but was fitted with a fluted bezel instead. This example features a beautifully preserved silver-finished dial with a simple “Swiss” designation at 6 o’clock, indicating an early execution. The dial is wonderfully restrained, with fine black printed text, elegant elongated hour markers and sharp dauphine hands that perfectly capture the understated sophistication of the period. The watch is fitted with its original Rolex leather strap and Rolex plaque buckle, completing the watch beautifully. Both the watch and its automatic calibre have been fully serviced by our watchmaker and are ready to be enjoyed for many years to come. An oversized dress Rolex from a more elegant era and quietly luxurious, exactly as early Rolex intended! title: Audemars Piguet Royal-Oak ref. 56175ST White Dial
description: It’s no exaggeration to say that the Audemars Piguet Royal Oak is one of the most iconic watches ever created. Designed in 1972 by Gérald Genta, the Royal Oak fundamentally changed the perception of the steel sports watch. For the first time, stainless steel was elevated to the level of a precious metal, finished by hand with intricate brushing, polishing, and beveling techniques. Its bold design—most notably the eight exposed hexagonal bezel screws—was revolutionary, turning functional elements into defining aesthetic features. Unsurprisingly, the Royal Oak became the signature model of Audemars Piguet and remains one of the most sought-after watches in the world. While the original Ref. 5402 was a time-only piece, Audemars Piguet quickly expanded the collection, introducing a variety of complications and iterations within the same distinctive case design. This brings us to the example offered here: the Royal Oak “Midsize” Ref. 56175ST. These models capture the essence of the original Ref. 5402 “Jumbo,” but in a more compact and refined 33mm case—offering a sleeker, highly wearable take on the classic design. This particular example remains in fantastic overall condition. The case and bracelet retain strong factory finishing throughout, with only light, even wear visible across the bezel, case, and bracelet. The white “petite tapisserie” dial is equally well preserved, featuring a clean surface. The lume filled indices have aged evenly, and the hands show a nicely matching tone. The integrated bracelet remains unmolested—always something we like to see. Powered by a quartz movement, the watch has been fully checked and fitted with a fresh battery, ready for daily wear. We’ve always felt these ultra-thin, midsize Oaks are somewhat underappreciated. On the wrist, they wear incredibly well—balanced, comfortable, and effortlessly cool. A sleeper in the Royal Oak world, don’t overlook this one! title: Rolex Datejust Turn-o-Graph 6309 Silver OCC Dial
description: The Rolex Turn-O-Graph, launched in 1953, is historically significant as the brand’s first serially produced watch to feature a rotating timing bezel. It predates more famous “tool watches” such as the Rolex Submariner and the Rolex GMT-Master, effectively serving as the blueprint for Rolex’s modern sports watch lineage. In 1954, Rolex transitioned the model into the Rolex Datejust family, adding the signature date window and Cyclops lens, blending tool-watch functionality with dress-watch elegance. By the end of the 1950s, the Turn-O-Graph had firmly established its distinctive identity within the collection. In the late 1950s, the United States Air Force Thunderbirds aerobatic squadron adopted the watch for navigational calculations. This earned the model its enduring nickname, “Thunderbird,” particularly in the North American market. At hand we have a lovely 36 mm steel-cased reference 6309 with a very attractive case and bezel. The silver dial features several less common details, including a “dot dot ” applied minute track, a “Swiss” only designation, and “Officially Certified Chronometer” printed in black. It is further enhanced by a roulette date display, fine dauphine hands, and elegant hour markers. The watch is paired with an American-made so-called “coffee bean” style Jubilee bracelet with end links marked 50, all in nice condition. The watch and its slow-setting movement have been fully serviced by our watchmaker. We feel the Turn-O-Graph adds a sportier edge to the classic dress watch execution. Slightly underappreciated at the moment, these models certainly deserve consideration. Do get back to us to discuss this compelling example further. title: IWC Ingenieur Automatic SL Khanjar Dial ref 3505 Steel and Gold Execution
description: The IWC Ingenieur has always been a watch shaped by its time. Originally conceived by IWC’s technical director, Albert Pellaton, the Ingenieur was developed as a response to the growing presence of electronics. Its soft-iron inner cage—essentially a Faraday cage—protected the movement from magnetism, while the efficient Pellaton winding system ensured reliability. A true technical watch, created with purpose. By the 1970s, the watch world was changing. In the midst of the Quartz Crisis, IWC turned to Gérald Genta to reimagine the Ingenieur. The result was the Ingenieur SL—a bold evolution, introducing an industrial aesthetic with a distinctive bezel, integrated bracelet, and a design language that still feels remarkably modern today. The example presented here, reference 3505, captures that spirit beautifully. Executed in a combination of steel and 18k gold, the 34 mm case wears larger than its dimensions might suggest—thanks to the slim bezel, expansive dial, and integrated bracelet. The case remains in lovely, strong condition, featuring a screw-down case back and the signature “fish” crown. The IWC-signed H-link bracelet is equally well preserved and comfortably fits wrists up to approximately 18 cm, completing the watch’s balanced and cohesive presence. But it is the dial that truly draws you in. Finished in a beautiful champagne tone, it features a distinctive “paper graph” motif that plays subtly with the light. Black-topped, boat-shaped hour markers, crisp black printed text, and a date at 3 o’clock keep the design both functional and elegant, while the lume has aged to a warm cream hue—adding just the right amount of character. What truly sets this watch apart, however, is the presence of the Khanjar and crossed-swords crest—the national emblem of Oman. This particular piece was commissioned by Sultan Qaboos bin Said, who ruled Oman from 1970 until 2020. Known for his appreciation of fine watches, the Sultan frequently ordered special pieces directly from manufacturers to present to close advisors, dignitaries, and friends. Watches bearing this royal insignia are among the most sought-after in the world of vintage collecting—combining rarity, provenance, and a unique historical connection. Powered by the automatic calibre 375 and fully serviced by our watchmaker, this Ingenieur is ready to be worn and appreciated for years to come. We have always had a soft spot for watches with a story—and this is exactly the kind of piece that continues to fascinate.You can read more about these over HERE A rare Oman-signed Ingenieur—get in touch to discover this exceptional piece. title: Rolex Submariner 5513 MilSub with Rolex Service Card
description: Say “MilSub” to a watch collector and you might just see them go weak at the knees—or momentarily lost for words. The Rolex Military Submariner, or “MilSub,” was produced in the 1970s in extremely small quantities (just over 1,000 pieces), of which only a few hundred are believed to survive today. It’s no surprise these watches are considered true grails—often commanding between €200k and €500k depending on condition and provenance. But what’s the story behind these rare Submariners? Although produced in the 1970s, the MilSub’s origins trace back to World War II and the rise of underwater combat operations. The Italians were early pioneers in this field, using Panerai watches—interestingly manufactured by Rolex at the time. Great Britain soon followed, commissioning purpose-built watches capable of withstanding prolonged underwater missions. After the war, this evolved into ongoing diving operations within the British Ministry of Defence (MoD), which required reliable, purpose-built wristwatches. By 1957, the MoD approached Rolex with a clear set of specifications. Rolex responded by adapting its Submariner with fixed spring bars, a larger bezel for operation with gloves, and dials using tritium lume (marked by the encircled “T”) instead of the more hazardous radium. For a brief period, the MoD turned to Omega, commissioning military versions of the Seamaster 300. However, in the early 1970s, they returned to Rolex—likely due to the superior water resistance offered by Rolex’s screw-down crown system. One requirement carried over from the Omega designs was the use of “sword hands,” providing greater lume surface and improved underwater legibility. The Submariner reference 5513 was subsequently modified to meet these specifications. Changes included fixed bars, a fully graduated 60-minute bezel insert, a matte dial, and the distinctive sword hands. Over the course of the 1970s, these watches underwent subtle evolutions. Early examples were marked “5513” between the lugs, followed by versions bearing both “5513” and “5517,” with the final series marked solely “5517.” These watches were issued primarily to two branches of the British military: Army (W10 designation) Case backs were engraved with: W10 (MoD code for the Army) 6645 (NATO code for time-measuring instruments) 99 (NATO country code for the UK) 923-7697 (NATO code for divers’ watches) Broad Arrow marking Issue number and year Royal Navy (0552 designation) Case backs were engraved with: 0552 (Royal Navy service code) 923-7697 (NATO code for divers’ watches) Broad Arrow marking Issue number and year Inside the case back of a correct MilSub, one would typically find engravings indicating the model reference, the date of manufacture for the case or case back, and—depending on the batch—the serial number matching the case. title: Patek Philippe Calatrava 3820J Silver Breguet Dial Tear Drop Lugs in 18k Yellow Gold
description: Introduced in 1932 during the Great Depression, the Patek Philippe Calatrava Ref. 96 was heavily influenced by the German Bauhaus principle of “form follows function.” As Patek Philippe’s first serially produced wristwatch, it played a crucial role in the company’s survival by offering a modern, minimalist alternative to ornate pocket watches. Named after the 12th-century Spanish Order of Calatrava, it remains the benchmark for the classic dress watch. Here we have exactly that spirit—a true Calatrava rooted in Bauhaus ideals, where form follows function—executed in 18k yellow gold. The 32 mm case features a screw-down case back and elegant “teardrop” (or “drop”) lugs, a detail that distinguishes it from many other Calatrava references and adds a subtle touch of sophistication to the profile. The silvered dial is beautifully balanced, showcasing applied Breguet-style numerals—one of our favourite details—paired with matching Breguet-style hands without lume. Sigma markings flank the six o’clock position, and an applied dot minute track frames the dial with some “pop”. This classic example is fitted with a handsome Bulang & Sons leather strap. An original 18k Patek pin buckle is included in the sale as well. It is powered by the hand-wound Patek Philippe caliber 177, fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker, and ready to be enjoyed for years to come. If you appreciate understated elegance and historically important design, this Calatrava deserves your attention. Get in touch with us for further details or to arrange a viewing! title: Omega Speedmaster 105.003 Ed White Tropical Brown Dial Pulsations Bezel with Extract of the Archives
description: The Omega Speedmaster, first introduced in 1957, remains one of the most iconic chronographs ever produced. While it later earned the legendary “Moonwatch” nickname for its role in space exploration, it was originally designed with motorsport in mind. This particular reference holds special historical importance as the last of the straight-lug Speedmasters, and notably the model worn by Ed White. During the Gemini 4 mission, White became the first American to perform a spacewalk, wearing a Speedmaster reference 105.003 as he exited the capsule using a hand-held oxygen jet maneuvering gun. The watch itself is in very good overall condition. It features an especially rare and desirable pulsations bezel—designed for measuring heart rate in beats per minute—which has developed a beautiful copper-brown fade over time, perfectly complementing the dial. The dial has aged into a rich milk-chocolate tone and retains all the correct details: “T SWISS T” markings, absence of the “Professional” text, and long hour markers. This warm, characterful Speedmaster is fitted on an Omega bracelet reference 7912, with end links marked “6” and a clasp stamped “3 65.” It is accompanied by an Omega Extract of the Archives confirming production in 1967 and delivery to the UK. The watch has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready to be enjoyed. We’re passionate about Speedmasters and always happy to talk about them. You’re very welcome to visit us at our lounge, or we can arrange a call at your convenience. title: Rolex Sea-Dweller 1665 Great White MK1 Dial with Box and Papers
description: The Rolex Sea-Dweller Reference 1665, introduced around 1967, was developed for saturation diving, featuring a groundbreaking helium escape valve and 2,000 ft (610 m) water resistance. Known for the “Double Red” (DRSD) dial from 1967–1977, it later transitioned to the all-white “Great White” dial before being replaced in the 1980s by the reference 16660 sapphire crystal model. Interestingly, “Great Whites” tend to trade at significantly lower prices than Double Reds, despite being produced for only about five years versus the roughly ten-year run of the Double Red! This particular example features a Mark I “Great White” dial variant—and what a lovely dial it is, with warm cream-coloured lume on both plots and hands. The iconic case remains in strong overall condition, with thick lugs and crown guards, complemented by a beautiful glossy black bezel insert. It is finished with a reference 93150 Oyster bracelet, fitted with end links marked 585 and a clasp stamped S DE 10. The watch comes complete with its iconic Rolex “Seahorse” outer box, inner box, paper holder, calendar card, and the original punched warranty papers showing a sale in 1978. The movement has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. We love these early Sea-Dwellers for their greater wrist presence compared to the Submariner. Let us help you add this iconic professional diver’s watch to your collection—contact us today! title: Rolex GMT-Master 1675/8 Unqiue Brown Khanjar Nipple Dial with Rolex Service Papers
description: The Rolex GMT-Master has always held a very special place in the world of collecting, but every now and then a watch appears that goes far beyond the already legendary status of the model itself. This 1974 Rolex GMT-Master 1675/8 is exactly that watch. At first glance, it already carries everything we love about the yellow gold GMT: the warm brown nipple dial, the rich brown insert, and the unmistakable glamour of one of Rolex’s most charismatic sports watches. It has that irresistible mix of elegance, confidence and jet-set charm that makes the gold GMT-Master such a fascinating watch in the metal. But what transforms this example into something truly extraordinary is the large Khanjar crest seen above 6 o’clock on the dial. From the 1970s onwards, Sultan Qaboos of Oman became known for personally commissioning watches that were enhanced with his royal emblem, the Khanjar crest, either on the dial or the caseback. Many of these watches were presented as gifts to trusted advisors, military figures and close relations, making them not only highly collectible, but also deeply historical objects. During the 1970s, many of these special-order watches were delivered to the Sultan through the family jeweller John Asprey of New Bond Street in London. Watches supplied through Asprey have become some of the most celebrated Omani-issued pieces in the world of collecting, with Rolex sports models standing out as the absolute rarest and most elusive examples to find with a Khanjar crest. Omani Khanjar-signed watches have long been among the most desirable and historically important special-order pieces in the world of vintage Rolex collecting. To find such a crest on any sports Rolex is exceptional. To find it on a gold GMT-Master 1675/8 is on another level entirely. In fact, this watch is believed to be one of only two known examples in this configuration, making it without question one of the rarest GMT-Masters in existence, and very possibly the rarest gold GMT-Master ever to surface. That alone would make it important, but in the metal it becomes something even more special. It is one of those watches that immediately stops you in your tracks. The striking combination of the warm matte brown dial with the bold Khanjar crest creates a presence that is impossible to ignore. It combines the elegance and jet-set appeal of the gold GMT with the mystique and historical importance of one of the most celebrated dial signatures in the vintage watch world. The result is not just rare, but breathtakingly attractive. Collectors will know just how elusive Khanjar watches are, especially when found on highly collectible Rolex sports models. This example stands at the very top of that category. Not only because of its rarity, but because watches like this almost never appear, and once they disappear into important collections, they are rarely seen again. It is exactly the kind of watch that lives in books, in stories, and in the dreams of seasoned collectors. Condition

title: Rolex Day-Date 18038 Purple Jubilee Dial Box and Papers French Hallmarks
description: Introduced in 1956, the Rolex Day-Date was the world’s first waterproof, self-winding chronometer wristwatch to display both the date and the day of the week—spelled out in full—across two separate windows. By 1977, Rolex updated the Day-Date with the introduction of the reference 18038, featuring the improved “single quickset” movement. This allowed the wearer to adjust the date independently of the time, alongside the transition from acrylic to sapphire crystal. This particular example stands out with a lovely, full case and crisp fluted bezel. Even better, it features small French hallmarks—subtle but important details indicating precious metal control marks for pieces destined for the French market at the time. The c18k yellow gold case remains in stunning condition, with the factory lines and curves still present. The President bracelet (ref. 8385) remains in strong condition, with tight links, solid end links, and a concealed clasp—exactly how you want to see it. It currently fits wrists up to approximately 18 cm. The dial is where things get especially interesting. This piece features a very cool “Jubilee” dial, with a repeating “Rolex Rolex” motif that creates a subtle three-dimensional effect. The tone leans toward a rare purple hue, combined with applied stick markers, gold printing, and a warm beige patina on the hour plots—giving it real depth and character. Interestingly, Rolex recently reintroduced a similar Jubilee-style motif on the Rolex Oyster Perpetual models, though in a more playful and colorful execution—making this vintage example feel even more relevant today. Adding to the charm, the day disc is in French, perfectly matching the French import hallmarks on the case. The original punched guarantee paper confirms a sale in Paris, tying the whole story together. The watch is accompanied by its original leather Rolex inner box, a polishing cloth, and a translation booklet—nice extras that complete the set. title: Rolex Day-Date 18038 Petrol Blue Stella Dial in 18k Yellow Gold
description: Since its official debut in 1956, the Day-Date has been produced exclusively in precious metals. As the flagship model of the Genevan manufacturer, it has long been synonymous with success, frequently seen on the wrists of heads of state, royalty, and other influential figures. Most Day-Dates were fitted with discreet, conservative dials—after all, most buyers of this model preferred elegance over extravagance. But Rolex made a notable exception to this unwritten rule: beginning in the 1970s, they introduced bright, high-gloss lacquered dials known today as “Stella” dials. At the time, these bold colours were not widely embraced, precisely because they attracted attention. As a result, production numbers remained low, making them highly sought after today. A wide array of colours is known—yellow, green, orange, red ,pink and multiple shades of blue, some embellished with gemstones, and produced across different metals. They also span various generations: from the early 18xx “pie-pan” versions to the later 18xxx models with flat dials. The manufacturing process was exceptionally labor-intensive. Each dial was spray-painted and then oven-baked to set the pigment. This cycle was repeated multiple times until the desired depth of colour and finish was achieved. Afterward, the dial was polished and coated with a clear lacquer. This final step created the distinctive effect where the printed text appears to float above the glossy surface, reminiscent of the porcelain-dial Daytonas. The example at hand is a second-series reference 18038, featuring a light blue—what we prefer to call “petrol blue”—Stella dial with diamond-set hour markers, and beautifully aged yellow tritium plots and fine gold printed text.The day disc is in English. The case is in outstanding condition, boasting thick, well-defined lugs and a sharp fluted bezel. The President bracelet is a reference 8385 with solid end links, a concealed clasp, and fits wrists up to 18 cm.This cool blue Day-Date has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. Add this exceptional petrol blue Stella to your collection — contact us to make it yours ! The 36mm yellow gold case remains robust, complemented by a well-defined 18k fluted bezel. The rare matte “Lemon” dial is in excellent condition, featuring crisp black text and Roman numerals, perfectly matched by the original black hands. All Buckley dials carry an additional element of rarity, though most examples encountered are white matte versions. The coloured Buckley dials are among the rarest; this “lemon” variant—resembling the tone of the ultra-rare exotic gold dials seen on hand-wound Daytona references—is, in our opinion, one of the finest, adding yet another dimension of rarity. It is fitted on a lovely Bulang & Sons leather strap with a Rolex plaque pin buckle. Furthermore, the watch comes with a rare Rolex inner box, calendar card, two booklets, and a Rolex cardholder. The original guarantee paper indicates a sale in 1984. The watch, along with its quickset movement, has been fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker. It is the only model remaining from the original “Air” series, which also included the Air-Lion, Air-Giant, and Air-Tiger. The example presented here is an early 1960s Reference 5504 in a lovely 36 mm size. The case remains in strong condition and is fitted with a smooth bezel that retains beautiful definition. Furthermore, the presence of “Swiss” only at the six o’clock position is correct for this era, complemented by elegant hour markers and fine dauphine hands. We have paired this quietly iconic watch with one of our straight end-link bracelets, which we feel is an excellent match. This Air-King has been checked and regulated by our watchmaker and is ready to be enjoyed for many years to come. It wears beautifully on the wrist—classic and under-the-radar, never loud. title: Rolex Explorer 1016 MK5 Dial Box and Paper Set
description: With its simple three-hand layout and comfortably wearable proportions, the Rolex Explorer stands apart from the Crown’s other sport watches. More than most, it walks the fine line between a true tool watch and an understated dress watch. At the same time, it was designed with clarity and durability in mind, built to remain highly legible even in harsh conditions and exposed environments. The Explorer was introduced in 1953, the same year as the Submariner and the Turn-O-Graph. Calling it a landmark year for Rolex—and for the watch industry as a whole—would hardly do it justice. These releases helped define the brand Rolex would become, shaped the modern sports watch segment, and laid the foundation for the collecting hierarchy that Rolex still dominates today. Presented here is a beautiful reference 1016, one of the final Explorer generations to feature tritium lume, matte dial variants, and a plexiglass crystal. This particular example shows a strong case and features a Mk5 dial, preserved in excellent condition. The lume has aged gracefully to an attractive creamy tone. The watch is fitted with a reference 78360 Oyster bracelet, with end links marked 580 and a clasp stamped I8. title: Rolex GMT-master 1675/3 Black Nipple Dial Steel and Gold "John Bull" Finish
description: The Rolex GMT-Master was launched in 1955 as an essential tool for pilots during the dawn of the jet age. Developed in collaboration with Pan American World Airways (Pan Am), it allowed pilots to track two time zones simultaneously using an extra 24-hour hand and a rotating bezel! At hand we have a reference 1675, one of the longest-running Rolex references. It replaced Bakelite with aluminum and introduced crown guards. This particular example boasts some rare and unusual features. The steel and gold case (or “Rolesor” in Rolex terminology) was hand-finished and engraved by the Bahamian official Rolex retailer John Bull, by their master watchmaker Günter Miethe. This customization was a popular request from the Bahamian retailer throughout the 1970s and 1980s. It is understood that Rolex gave their blessing to John Bull to carry out these modifications for the local market, resulting in this distinctive “bark” texture. The Jubilee bracelet is also engraved with this texture and represents the typical US-market variant, featuring oval links with 14k gold trim and a gold crown on the clasp. The black dial is in excellent condition and features the desirable nipple-style hour markers, an applied coronet, gold printed text, and a beautifully even lume patina across both the hands and hour plots. The watch has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. At first glance, the combination of steel and gold with the bark finish can be somewhat challenging. However, over time, we have come to truly appreciate its unique character—not to mention the remarkable level of craftsmanship required to execute such a finish. Not long ago, these pieces tended to trade at a discount compared to standard examples of the same reference, but that narrative has gradually shifted. As the “John Bull Special” secures its place within the Rolex collecting world—and as more collectors recognize it as something far beyond a typical aftermarket customization—values have steadily continued to rise. If you are looking for something far removed from the standard Rolex sports catalog, this true conversation piece could be a perfect contender! title: Audemars Piguet Tank Style Dresswatch Cream dial in 18 k rose gold
description: Founded in 1875 in Le Brassus, Switzerland, by Jules Louis Audemars and Edward Auguste Piguet, Audemars Piguet is best known today for its defining Royal Oak collection. However, prior to 1972, the company focused primarily on high-complication movements, developing, for example, one of the first minute-repeater wristwatches as early as 1892. From the 1930s onwards, Audemars Piguet also became renowned for its ultra-thin dress watches. These movements were exceptionally difficult to design and produce, serving as a true demonstration of technical mastery—allowing elegant timepieces to achieve remarkable slimness and refinement. Presented here is a charming pre-reference, “Tank-style” dress watch in 18k rose gold. The case, measuring 25 × 29 mm including the lugs, remains in lovely condition. It features a snap-on case back and a small, discreet winding crown. The dial is executed in a matte cream tone, with painted black Roman numerals and simple rose gold stick hands. In keeping with its minimalist design, there is no lume and no seconds hand, enhancing its understated elegance. The watch is fitted with an original Audemars Piguet leather strap, and its hand-wound ultra-thin movement has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. For those who appreciate the quiet mastery of early Audemars Piguet! title: Rolex Cosmograph Daytona Tropical Dial ref. 6239
description: Rolex has more iconic wristwatches than any other brand when you think about it—from the archetype of the dive watch, the Submariner, to the President’s watch, the Day-Date, to the pilot’s watch created for Pan American Airways, the GMT-Master. Add the Explorer, the Datejust… yet no model from the Genevan manufacturer captures collectors’ hearts quite like the Daytona! The watch we now know as the Daytona didn’t actually debut under that name. When Rolex introduced the model in 1963, it carried only the title “Cosmograph.” It wasn’t until the following year that “Daytona” appeared on the dial—while Cosmograph remained, a pairing that continues today. Cosmograph has always been an unusual term. We’re familiar with chronograph, derived from the Greek chronos (time) and graphein (to write), referring to early timing devices like Nicolas Rieussec’s 1821 instrument that physically marked elapsed time on paper. Cosmograph, however, wasn’t coined by Rolex. The word blends cosmo (world or universe) with the same “writing/recording” root. It also recalls cosmography, the field dedicated to describing the structure of the world—and later the wider universe. On offer today is an early and highly desirable example of this legendary model: a reference 6239 featuring a 37 mm stainless-steel case in excellent condition, paired with a crisp polished steel bezel engraved with a 300 “Units Per Hour” scale and “pump “ style chrono pushers. And the star of the show: A silver dial whose originally black sub-dials have aged into a warm chestnut-brown tone—a stunning tropical transformation created purely by time. This kind of natural ageing gives the watch true individuality; no two tropical dials evolve in the same way. The dial also features black-printed “Rolex Cosmograph Daytona” text and T-SWISS-T at the bottom, all set against a beautifully preserved soleil-finished silver backdrop. The watch is fitted with a Rolex folded Oyster bracelet, reference 7835, with 557 end links. The watch and its hand-wound calibre 722 movement have been fully serviced by our watchmaker. If you’re looking for a chronograph with history, style, charisma—and an unmistakably unique warm presence—this is the one. Get in touch with us! title: Rolex Cosmograph Daytona 6239 Black Dial
description: Introduced in 1963, the Rolex Cosmograph Daytona was designed with race car drivers in mind, featuring a tachymeter bezel to measure average speeds. Initially slow to gain traction, its popularity surged through Rolex’s partnership with the Daytona International Speedway. The “Daytona” name began appearing on dials around 1964–1965, strengthening its connection to the legendary Daytona 500. Over time, the model evolved from hand-wound movements to automatic calibres in 1988, and later to contemporary ceramic bezels—yet it has remained one of the most sought-after chronographs ever produced. Presented here is a stainless steel reference 6239, featuring an excellent case, a polished steel tachymeter bezel, and correct non-screw-down “pump” pushers. The black dial is equally well preserved, with crisp white “Rolex Cosmograph Daytona” printing on the upper half, complemented by clean silvered sub-dials. The lume has aged to a rich mustard tone across both the hour plots and hands, and the dial is signed “T Swiss T” at 6 o’clock. The watch is fitted with a U.S.-market “C&I” Oyster bracelet, featuring spring-loaded links and a clasp stamped 1/66. title: Rolex GMT-Master 1675 Tropical Brown Gilt Chapter Ring Dial Pointed Crown Guard Case
description: The Rolex GMT-Master ref. 1675 is one of the most iconic and recognisable vintage watches ever produced. Manufactured from 1959 to 1980, its 21-year production run makes it one of the longest-standing references in Rolex history, resulting in a wide array of highly collectible variations. The model achieved legendary status through its association with Pan Am pilots, and NASA astronaut Jack Swigert of the Apollo 13 mission. Presented here is an early 1960s example featuring the so-called pointed crown guard (PCG) case. The Pointed Crown Guards, often referred to by Italian collectors as “Cornino” (“little horns”), are a hallmark of the earliest GMT-Master 1675 models produced between 1959 and approximately 1964. When Rolex transitioned from the original ref. 6542—which had no crown guards—to the 1675, it experimented with case shapes. The PCG is the most sought-after case design of that period. Unlike the thicker, rounded crown guards found on later models, the PCG tapers to a sharp point, giving the watch a slimmer and more technical aesthetic. The case remains in excellent condition, retaining strong proportions. It is fitted with a slightly faded red-back “Pepsi” bezel insert, where the blue has softened to a washed, denim-like tone, while the red remains vibrant. The dial is a so-called gilt “chapter ring” version. Unlike later dials, where the text is printed on the surface, a gilt dial is created through a complex galvanic process. The dial begins as a brass plate, with the text and markings applied in clear lacquer. It is then treated so that the surrounding surface turns black, leaving the underlying brass visible—resulting in a rich, glossy, gold-toned text with remarkable depth. The “chapter ring” refers to the fine gilt circle connecting the minute markers along the outer edge of the dial. Over time, this example has developed a warm brown patina—referred to by collectors as a “tropical dial.” A highly sought-after characteristic, it gives the watch a unique warmth and character. Furthermore, the dial features smaller lume plots, typical for chapter ring versions, with correct period lume that reacts with a bright green glow under UV light—present both on the dial and the handset. The watch is fitted with a folded Jubilee bracelet, reference 6251H, with end links stamped 55 and a clasp dated 1/65. The watch has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready for many years of enjoyment. For days in the sun and nights across time zones , chocolate tropical dial made for sunshine and stories yet to be written, get in touch ! title: Rolex Day-Date 1807 Bark Finish Champagne Dial in 18k Yellow Gold Box and Paper Set
description: Introduced in 1956, the Rolex Day-Date was the world’s first wristwatch to display both the date and the day of the week spelled out in full. Known as the “President’s Watch,” it quickly became a symbol of prestige, crafted exclusively in precious metals such as gold and platinum. During the 1950s and 1960s, more expressive finishes in gold gained popularity. This led to the introduction of references such as the 1806, 1807, and 1811—distinct variations of the Day-Date featuring decorative textures on bezels, bracelet center links, and in the case of the 1811, even the case itself. The reference 1807 is particularly notable for its characteristic bark-finished bezel. The example offered here is a wonderful representation of that era, reaffirming our appreciation for the charm of four-digit Day-Date references. The 36 mm case remains in pleasing condition, complemented by a beautifully hand-finished bezel that adds depth and character. The classic pie-pan dial is presented in an elegant champagne tone, featuring applied stick hour markers, crisp black printed text, and softly aged lume plots that harmonize perfectly with the hands. A Spanish day disc adds an extra layer of character and provenance. This Day-Date is fitted on a refined Bulang & Sons leather strap and is accompanied by its original guarantee papers, indicating a sale in Spain in 1975. It further includes its inner and outer Rolex boxes, the original plastic document holder, and a period-correct Rolex calendar card.The watch and its slow setting movement have been fully serviced by our watch maker. A beautifully preserved and characterful example—one that captures the essence of vintage Rolex at its most expressive ! title: Rolex Explorer II  16550 Galaxy Tropical Dial with Box and Papers
description: The Rolex Explorer II is a true no-nonsense tool watch, originally introduced in 1971 as reference 1655 and nicknamed “Freccione” for its bold orange 24-hour hand. Designed for cave explorers, it allowed the wearer to distinguish between day and night in complete darkness. Presented here is a later reference 16550. This model addressed the limited commercial success of the 1655 by introducing a more conventional aesthetic, aligning it more closely with the GMT-Master and Submariner. The quirky dial of the 1655 was replaced with a cleaner layout featuring Mercedes hands, circular and baton hour markers, and applied 18k white gold surrounds. More importantly, the 16550 introduced the calibre 3085, allowing the 24-hour hand to be set independently of the main hour hand—transforming it into a true GMT watch capable of tracking two time zones simultaneously. The reference 16550 is considered a transitional model, produced for only a short period between approximately 1985 and 1989—an unusually brief lifespan for Rolex—making it both rare and highly collectible. It was also the first Explorer II to be offered with a white “Polar” dial. The present example features a black dial that has aged in a particularly attractive and sympathetic way, resembling the milky way. The lume has developed a warm cream tone, while portions of the dial have taken on a subtle brown hue, creating a unique effect reminiscent of “snake skin”—a striking and highly individual character. The case remains in lovely condition, as does the characteristic thick-font bezel. The watch is fitted with a reference 93150 Oyster bracelet, with 593 end links and a clasp stamped I11. Furthermore, this example comes with an additional black dial and handset, both with tritium lume, allowing the option to return the watch to a more traditional appearance if desired. The watch is accompanied by its original Rolex inner box, an Explorer booklet, and the typical U.S.-stamped guarantee paper, indicating a sale in 1988.Historically, the Explorer II has been considered an underdog within the Rolex sports family, yet it is so stylish and appealing. These watches make the perfect summer watch… Where tool watch heritage meets wild, organic character , Contact us to secure this exceptional Explorer II. title: Breguet Classique Calendrier Day Date moon phase Guilloché dial in 18 k white gold ref 7337BB
description: The story of Breguet begins in 1775, when Abraham-Louis Breguet founded his workshop on the Quai de l’Horloge in Paris. Widely regarded as the greatest watchmaker of all time, Breguet was not merely a manufacturer, but an inventor whose ideas laid the foundations of modern horology. During the late 18th and early 19th centuries, Breguet introduced an astonishing number of breakthroughs. Among his most important inventions are the tourbillon—patented in 1801 to improve accuracy—the Breguet overcoil hairspring, pare-chute shock protection, and the instantly recognizable Breguet hands and numerals. Many of these innovations remain fundamental to watchmaking today. Breguet quickly became the watchmaker of choice for royalty, scientists, and military leaders, with clients including Marie Antoinette, Napoleon Bonaparte, Tsar Alexander I, and Winston Churchill. At hand, we have a truly typical Breguet creation: an 18k white gold calendar model housed in a 39mm coin-edge case, fitted with elegant straight lugs. The case back features a sapphire crystal, allowing a clear view of the movement, while discreet correctors on the case sides are used to adjust the complications. The absolute star of this watch is the dial. It features a silvered solid 18k white gold dial executed with multiple contrasting guilloché patterns. The main dial displays a very fine concentric criss-cross texture, while the time sub-dial is decorated with a unique cross-weave basket pattern. A small seconds sub-dial adds contrast with a linear sunburst finish. Time is indicated on an off-centered dial with painted Roman numeral hour markers, complemented by a small off-center seconds sub-dial at the 5 o’clock position. Carefully executed cut-outs accommodate the calendar and moon-phase displays. And speaking of the moon phase—just look at the beautifully hand-engraved moon, finished to perfection! Completing the look are the signature blued Breguet hands, delivering a flawless 10/10 in style. The watch is powered by the automatic Breguet Caliber 502.3 QSE1, featuring a guilloché-textured rotor. The rotor is partially recessed into the movement, contributing to the slim profile, and the watch has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. description: Some watches quietly define a brand’s story—and the Tudor Submariner Reference 7928 is undoubtedly one of them. Introduced at the end of the 1950s, this reference marked an important step forward for Tudor. It was the first Submariner to feature crown guards, a subtle yet meaningful evolution inspired by the needs of professional divers, including those of the French Navy. A small detail perhaps, but one that speaks volumes about purpose and progress. This particular example immediately stands out thanks to its beautifully preserved case, still showing the elegant lines of the early “pointed crown guards.” There is something especially charming about these sharper, more defined guards—slightly more expressive than the later rounded versions, and a detail that collectors continue to appreciate. Then there is the bezel. Fitted with a rare “skinny 4” insert, believed to have been used only for a short period around 1963, it adds an extra layer of intrigue to the watch. Over time, it has developed a gentle fade—just enough to give it that honest, lived-in feel we so often look for. The kind of detail that doesn’t shout, but quietly tells its story. The dial remains in lovely condition, featuring a chapter ring and the desirable “Swiss only” designation at 6 o’clock. The hour markers have developed a warm yellow patina, beautifully echoed by the gilt-toned hands, creating a harmonious and characterful appearance. Fitted with one of our leather straps and powered by a movement that has been fully serviced by our watchmaker, this is a watch that feels both honest and complete. A piece with presence, history, and just the right amount of charm—exactly how we like to see them! title: Rolex Chronograph ref. 4500 Pulsations Dial
description: The Rolex Chronograph ref. 4500, introduced around 1945, is a historically significant and rare example of one of the earliest waterproof Oyster chronographs, often referred to as the “Monoblocco” due to its one-piece case design. It marked a key design shift toward the later “modern” Daytona, with a 36mm size, straight lugs, and a two-register layout powered by a Valjoux 23–based movement. The case on this example is in excellent condition, featuring a screw-down back, pump-style pushers, and its original winding crown still attached. The true showstopper is the extremely rare pulsations dial. The pulso meter scale was primarily designed for medical professionals, namely doctors. pulsations -signed Rolex chronographs are extraordinarily rare, with approximately 50 pieces known across all metals and references. The lineage of these “medical” chronographs includes very early references such as 2023 and 2057 from the late 1920s, the “Bariletto” reference 3525 in steel during the 1940s, as well as references 4048, 4062, 4099, 4537 (in stainless steel or stainless steel and gold), and the ref. 4500 in gold. In the 1950s, the pulso meter scale appeared on some examples of the 6034 and 6234 in stainless steel and gold, followed by a handful of ref. 6238s with the scale inside the indexes, and finally around ten examples of the ref. 6239 Daytona in steel. After this period, Rolex ceased producing pulsometer dials for over 60 years, and they remain absent in modern production. This particular dial features a French-language pulso meter scale in blue (‘Gradué pour 30 pulsations’), suggesting it was likely made on special request for a French-speaking doctor. Such dials were extremely rare and difficult to produce in batches due to very low demand. The dial also features a fine black seconds track, two sub dials, applied Arabic numerals, and arrowhead hour markers, all showing pleasing patina. It is topped with clean non-lumed stick-style hands, completing a truly remarkable vintage chronograph. The watch is paired with an Oyster riveted-style ref 7206 bracelet, with end links marked 60 and a clasp stamped 1/60. This fine doctor’s chronograph and its hand-wound Cal. VLJX.23 movement have been fully serviced by our watchmaker. One of approximately 50 known pulsations examples across all references and a true collector’s find—reach out to secure this rare doctor’s Rolex chronograph! Specifications: Rolex Reference 4500 Chronograph 36 mm steel ”monobloc” case, pump style pushers, early winding crown Serial 499xxx dating to 1946 Super rare pulso meter/pulsations dial, no lume, two sub dials Oyster bracelet reference 7207 with 60 end links Valjoux 23 based hand -wound movement Fully serviced by our watchmaker 1 year mechanical warranty Free worldwide Fedex Priority shipping

Because this watch was made at the height of the Art Deco period, the design was considered very avant garde – not too different to how it was perceived over 70 years later when the ref. 5099 was launched! Three examples of the original “Cabriolet” were made between 1927 and 1929. The 18 k Rose gold case features two white gold “stripes” and “merge into the lugs”. title: Rolex Turn-O-Graph ref 6202
description: The Rolex Turn-O-Graph ref. 6202, introduced in 1953, is often called the “grandfather” of the modern Rolex sports watch. While the Submariner receives most of the fame, the 6202 Turn-O-Graph was actually the first mass-produced Rolex to feature a rotating bezel. It established the design language later seen in the Submariner, GMT-Master, and the early Milgauss. Before the 6202, Rolex watches were mostly elegant Oyster Perpetuals; this model marked their first true “tool watch”. The 60-minute graduated bezel allowed users to track elapsed time, a revolutionary feature for professional timing at the time. This example features a lovely 36 mm case with a bezel insert fitted with the distinctive red triangle at the 12 o’clock position. It is paired with a riveted Oyster bracelet, reference 7206, with end links marked 58 and a clasp stamped 1/69. The gilt dial is in lovely condition and fitted with a gilt-coloured handset, including the distinctive “big lollipop” seconds hand. Often overlooked, yet full of character, these Turn-O-Graphs deserve a closer look. Get in touch to make it yours. description: Presented in 1932, amid the challenges of the Great Depression, the Patek Philippe Calatrava Ref. 96 signaled a new direction for Patek Philippe. Drawing clear inspiration from the Bauhaus doctrine of “form follows function,” it became the firm’s first serially produced wristwatch and introduced a restrained, contemporary aesthetic at a time when ornate pocket watches still dominated. Named after the 12th-century Spanish Order of Calatrava, it has since come to define the archetype of the classic dress watch. The example offered here captures that philosophy perfectly—a quintessential Calatrava shaped by clarity, proportion, and understatement—crafted in 18k yellow gold. The 32 mm case is fitted with a screw-down case back and elegant teardrop lugs, a graceful design element that distinguishes it from many other references and lends the watch a refined, flowing silhouette. Its silvered dial is harmoniously laid out, featuring applied Breguet-style numerals—an especially charming detail—complemented by matching Breguet-style hands without lume. Sigma signatures flank the six o’clock position, while an applied dot minute track subtly enlivens the overall composition. The watch is paired with a lovely leather strap, and an original 18k Patek Philippe pin buckle attached . This fine Calatrava is acompanied with its original certificate of origine, stating a sale in 1989 and 2 booklets and its leather paper holder. The watch and its hand wound cal. 177 has been fully serviced and by our watch maker. For those who appreciate the poetry of Breguet numerals and the elegance of teardrop lugs—do get in touch to discuss! title: Rolex Submariner “Kermit” Flat 4 with Pouch and Papers Ref. 16610LV
description: The Rolex Submariner was first introduced in 1953, and to commemorate the iconic dive watch’s 50th anniversary, Rolex launched the 16610LV in 2003. Featuring the signature 'LV' (Lunette Verte) green bezel, this model also boasts a glossy dial with enlarged hour markers and slightly thicker hands—collectively known as the 'Maxi dial.' While this was the first time Rolex officially used the term 'Maxi,' collectors had been referring to the wider markers on 1970s matte-dial Subs for years prior. This particular piece is in outstanding condition, with both the case and dial showing minimal signs of wear, and the green case back sticker still attached. The green bezel insert remains remarkably free of scratches or blemishes, preserving its vibrant color. This version is an early one, where the "four" on the bezel is flattened, earning it the nickname "Flat Four." The solid-link Oyster bracelet, reference 93250, is also in excellent shape, complete with a “AD 11" stamped clasp and solid end links. The dial is from an early generation as well. Notable details include the unique 'flattened O' in the Rolex lettering at 12 o'clock, where the character takes on an oval shape. At 6 o'clock, the minute markers at the 28 and 32 positions are shorter than on later generations, and the 'Swiss Made' text extends beyond these markers. This distinctive feature is referred to as 'Wide' or Mk1 typography. The watch comes with its paper holder, a booklet, and its original punched warranty paper stating a sale in 2004, Iran, plus a green Rolex pouch. This lovely Submariner is fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker. We believe these watches are quickly becoming "classics" and make for excellent everyday wearers. Get in touch! title: Patek Philippe Calatrava 3569G Hammered Dial in 18k White Gold Back Winder
description: One of the true cornerstones of the Patek Philippe collection is its classic dress watch—the Patek Philippe Calatrava. Every great maison needs a pure, time-only dress watch as its foundation, and with the Calatrava, Patek has upheld and refined this tradition for nearly a century. To understand its significance, we look back to the 1930s, when Charles and Henri Stern acquired the company. Determined to reposition and modernise the manufacture, their first important release was the Calatrava. With the launch of the reference 96 in 1932—at a time when wristwatches were steadily replacing pocket watches—they laid the foundation for Patek Philippe’s enduring reputation as one of the world’s most respected watchmakers. Presented here is an exceptionally attractive Calatrava “Back Winder” reference 3569. It is housed in a sleek and slim 36 mm x 8.3 mm 18k white gold case, beautifully finished with an intricate hammered texture. The result is a case that radiates old-world charm, warmth, and artisanal character in equal measure. The winding crown is discreetly positioned on the caseback—hence the model’s charming nickname, “Back Winder.” The dial is equally captivating, finished with the same refined attention to detail. Understated baton hour markers filled with black enamel create a striking contrast against the textured surface. They are paired with slender stick-style hands, also accented in black enamel, resulting in a harmonious and highly sophisticated aesthetic that perfectly captures the Calatrava ethos. The watch is fitted with a handsome Bulang & Sons leather strap and powered by the automatic Patek Philippe caliber 350—one of Patek Philippe’s earliest movements to utilise a peripheral rotor. The movement has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready to be enjoyed for years to come. title: Rolex Oyster Perpetual Date Champagne dial Zephyr in 18 k yellow gold ref 1510
description: These slightly offbeat “Zephyr” models were introduced in the mid-1950s, beginning with reference 6582, and were marketed—particularly in the United States—as the “world’s handsomest” and highly waterproof watch. The name was primarily used for the American market, giving the model a distinctive position within the broader Rolex Oyster Perpetual family. The most striking feature of reference 1510 is its hand-engraved, dappled bezel finish, often referred to as the “Morellis” finish. Each bezel was individually executed by hand, meaning no two examples are exactly alike—an unusual and charming detail within the typically restrained Rolex catalogue. The dial is divided into four quadrants by a fine crosshair motif and notably omits traditional hour markers—quite a departure from the classic layout normally found on Rolex dress watches of the period. This design gives the watch a slightly avant-garde, almost architectural presence on the wrist. The 34 mm 18k yellow gold case remains in fine condition, as does the beautifully preserved Morellis-finished bezel. The champagne-coloured dial is clean and elegant, free of lume on both dial and hands, maintaining a crisp and refined appearance, interrupted only by the practical date function. Fitted with a Bulang & Sons leather strap—an excellent match in our view—the watch has been fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker and is ready to be worn and appreciated. title: Patek Philippe World Time ref 5130R Guilloché Dial in 18k Rose Gold with Box and Papers
description: The story of the Patek Philippe World Time complication begins with Louis Cottier, a Geneva watchmaker who invented the modern world time mechanism in the early 1930s. His system allowed the wearer to read the time in 24 time zones simultaneously, using a central hour and minute hand, a rotating 24-hour ring, and a city ring representing major world cities. Patek Philippe quickly recognized the brilliance of Cottier’s invention and, in 1937, introduced its first world time wristwatch: Ref. 96 HU (“HU” for Heure Universelle ). Over the following decades, Patek Philippe refined the concept with several legendary references. The Ref. 1415 HU of the 1940s and 1950s featured a larger case and improved legibility, while the Ref. 2523 HU of the 1950s is celebrated for its breathtaking cloisonné enamel dials depicting world maps. Today, the Ref. 2523 is considered the holy grail of Patek Philippe World Time watches, with exceptional examples achieving tens of millions at auction. From the 1970s until around 2000, Patek Philippe produced very few World Time watches, until the introduction of Ref. 5110, which marked the beginning of the modern World Time era. This reference remains a favorite here at Bulang & Sons—an icon that perfectly balances technical ingenuity with timeless elegance. Presented here is its successor, Ref. 5130R, with the “R” denoting its 18k rose gold case. Slightly larger than its predecessor at 39.5mm, the watch features a classic Calatrava-style case and a pusher at 10 o’clock that advances the local time by one hour while simultaneously rotating the city and 24-hour rings, all without disturbing the minute hand. A sapphire display case back reveals the Caliber 240/228 HU, powered by a refined 22k gold micro-rotor. The hands are the distinctive knife style, with a sharp minute hand and an expressive bubble hour hand—an elegant nod to historic World Time models. The dial is a true work of art, adorned with a beautifully executed guilloché pattern in tones that harmonize effortlessly with the warmth of the rose gold case. The outer city ring displays 24 cities, each representing a different time zone, allowing the wearer to travel the world at a single glance. The watch is paired with a beautiful Bulang & Sons leather strap and fitted with its original 18k rose gold Patek Philippe folding buckle. It comes complete with inner and outer boxes, booklets, the leather document holder, and the original Certificate of Origin, confirming its sale in 2007. Fully serviced by our watchmaker, this exceptional world timer is ready for many years of faithful service. More than a watch, this is a companion for a life well travelled — quietly marking time across continents, memories, and moments. Get in touch to make it yours ! title: Rolex Oyster Perpetual Swiss only OCC Cream dial ref 6532
description: The Rolex Oyster Perpetual ref. 6532 was introduced in 1956 and discontinued in 1958, making it a relatively short-lived reference among these 34 mm Oyster Perpetuals. This example features a lovely case with a smooth, sporty-feeling bezel and a beautiful cream-coloured dial. The dial features applied Rolex lettering, arrowhead markers, “Swiss Only” text at six o’clock, and the “Officially Certified Chronometer” designation on the lower half of the dial all topped off by elegant dauphine hands . The warm tone of the dial combined with the sharp markers gives this watch a distinctly vintage character, something collectors of early Rolex Oyster models truly appreciate. We paired it with our own steel straight end-link bracelet, which gives the watch a very cool and period-correct look. The watch and its automatic movement have been fully serviced by our watchmaker and are running perfectly, ready for a new wrist. Altogether, a charming and honest early Rolex Oyster that captures the understated elegance of the period, get in touch ! title: Zenith S.58 Dive watch
description: The Zenith S.58 was introduced in 1958. The “S” is believed to stand for “Super” (water-resistant) or “Scaphandre” (French for diving suit), while “58” denotes the launch year. Some sources also suggest a connection to the Sikorsky S-58helicopter, although this has never been definitively proven. Regardless, it remains an important early—and definitely cool-looking—dive watch. The example presented here comes from the fourth and final generation, featuring a lovely 36.5 mm case and a rotating Bakelite bezel in equally nice condition. The black dial has a beautiful domed or “pie-pan” style, a date function at 3 o’clock, and lumed Arabic numerals at 6, 9, and 12. It is further complemented by the seldom-seen “sword”-shaped hands. The watch is paired with a nice one-pass strap, giving it a classically styled, utilitarian dive watch vibe. The automatic Cal. 2542PC has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready for many more years of enjoyment. A classic utilitarian diver ready for a new wrist—get in touch for the details ! title: Cartier Santos Dumont Ultra Thin ref. 96053 Paris Dial
description: The Cartier Santos-Dumont line was born when Louis Cartier designed a wristwatch for his pilot friend Alberto Santos-Dumont, who needed a hands-free way to check the time while flying. This reference pays faithful tribute to that original concept. This ref 96053 features an 18k yellow gold case measuring 23 × 23 mm (excluding the lugs), fitted with a blue cabochon on the winding crown. The watch has an incredibly slim profile—often described as “extra-flat” or “ultra-thin”—making it a highly refined dress watch. The dial is classic Cartier: white with black Roman numerals, a hidden Cartier signature at 10 o’clock, and a “Paris” signature at 6 o’clock. The “Paris” inscription indicates that the watch was sold through Cartier’s flagship boutique at 13 Rue de la Paix. In the early 1990s, Cartier transitioned to “Swiss Made” signatures, making “Paris” dials rarer and more desirable for collectors today. The watch is paired with the photographed leather strap, and its hand-wound Cal. 21 movement, based on an ébauche by Frédéric Piguet, has been fully serviced by our watchmaker. We’d love to share our passion for Cartier with you. Please feel free to arrange a visit or a call — we look forward to welcoming you! title: Vacheron Constantin Disco Volante in 18 k yellow gold ref 6508
description: Founded in 1755 by Jean-Marc Vacheron in Geneva, Vacheron Constantin is the world’s oldest watch manufacturer in continuous operation. Known as part of the "Holy Trinity" of Swiss watchmaking, the brand merged with François Constantin in 1819, adopting the motto: "Do better if possible, and that is always possible." With a lineage stretching back centuries, the brand has been responsible for some of the most sophisticated and beautifully executed complications ever produced. At hand, we have a very ultra-thin and strikingly cool “Disco Volante,” or flying saucer. The 34mm 18k yellow gold case features hidden lugs and a hand-finished guilloché surface with a glorious red patina on the gold. The silver-colored dial is elegantly simple, with stick markers, no lume, and a mirror-like finish that reflects the hands. The stick hands are topped by a black Vacheron & Constantin signature, completing the refined look. This rare flying saucer has been paired with a thin leather strap and a uniquely shaped 18k gold Vacheron Constantin pin buckle. The ultra-thin caliber 1003 hand-wound movement has been fully serviced by our watchmaker and is ready for years of enjoyment. We love these Disco Volantes—they are effortlessly cool, lightweight on the wrist, and an absolute joy to wear. Take a closer look, and let’s have a conversation about this remarkable piece. title: Rolex GMT-Master 16753 Root Beer Soleil Brown Tropical Dial in Steel and Gold
description: The Rolex GMT-Master is one of the most iconic sports watches and has always been a favorite among collectors, thanks to its variety of bezel and dial options. For us, one of the most striking combinations is the brown dial paired with the two-tone case. It exudes serious vintage vibes and offers a unique twist—something a little different that we love. This particular example is in fantastic condition, with a strong and well-preserved case. The dial is excellent, and the tritium in the hour markers and hands has developed a subtle beige patina over time. There is some oxidation around the markers, giving it a unique “tropical” appearance. The bezel insert has faded evenly, adding even more vintage character to this GMT-Master, with the original brown fading to an “old pink” tone we love. The two-tone Jubilee bracelet (reference 62523H) is in good condition, with a clasp stamped ‘I 4’ and reference 450 end links. This watch comes as a complete set, including the inner and outer boxes, paper holder, three booklets, and punched papers indicating a sale in Switzerland in 1987. The watch has been checked and regulated by our watchmaker and is in perfect working order. Curious about this piece? We’re happy to answer any questions. title: Patek Philippe Calatrava ref. 96 18k Yellow Gold
description: Introduced in 1932, the Patek Philippe Reference 96 is the original Calatrava, created during the Great Depression as a simpler, more accessible, and modern wristwatch. Designed by David Penney, its Bauhaus-inspired, minimalist aesthetic helped define the modern dress watch, featuring a 31 mm case, a clean dial, and a small seconds display. It was the first serially produced wristwatch to carry a formal Patek Philippe reference number. Produced from 1932 until 1973, it featured a sleek design with integrated lugs and a flat bezel, embodying the “form follows function” philosophy. Although considered a dress watch, it was produced in a wide variety of configurations, including stainless steel, precious metals, sector dials, and even luminous military-style variants. Presented here is a lovely 18k yellow gold example, with a beautifully preserved case and crisp, clearly visible gold hallmarks. The snap-on case back features an elegant, finely engraved motif—presumably a logo—whose origin remains unknown, adding a touch of mystery and charm. The dial is an opaline silver, with raised enamel signature and a “dot” minute track. It features a small running seconds at 6 o’clock, applied baton-style markers, and is completed by sharp dauphine hands. The watch is paired with the pictured strap and fitted with an AW-signed plaque buckle. The watch and its hand wound movement has been fully serviced by our watch maker. A true icon of understated elegance & A cornerstone piece for any serious collection , contact us to learn more about this fine 96 ! Fully serviced by our watchmaker One-year mechanical warranty Free worldwide Fedex Priority shipping

title: Rolex Explorer 5504 “Super precision” Gilt Chapter-ring Dial
description: The Explorer has long represented what many consider the perfect “one good watch” to own. Its clean design and understated versatility make it equally suited to everyday life and adventurous escapes. The reference 1016, in particular, holds a special place in watch history—produced for an impressive 29 years, from 1960 to 1989. With its modest 36mm case, no-date dial, and sharp black face—whether gilt or matte—it remains the quintessential vintage Rolex sports watch. Quietly iconic, the Explorer is the Rolex for those who appreciate substance over status. For many watch enthusiasts, including us at Bulang and Sons, a gilt dial Explorer with a chapter ring is the ultimate grail—and when people picture that, they usually think of the Rolex 1016. While this is not a reference 1016, it is the ref. 5504—often overlooked and perhaps even “a forgotten Explorer reference.” Unlike its “brother” reference 5500, which is 34mm, the 5504 retains the full 36mm case size. Let’s break down why this watch is such an interesting one: The 5504 is often mistaken for a 1016, and truth be told, the only real difference is that the 5504 was never chronometer-certified. At the time, this was Rolex’s way of offering a nearly identical model at a lower price point. (Or—depending on how you look at it—charging more for the 1016! 😉) When the 5504 Explorer was first produced in 1958, the chronometer-certified Explorer was actually the Rolex 6610, which used the older caliber 1030 movement that Rolex was beginning to phase out of its sports models. The 5504 was one of the first to feature the newer caliber 1530, which was also used in early Submariners of the time, including the legendary 5508 and 5510 “Big Crown” models—better known as the “James Bond” watches. This newer caliber was compact and highly precise, but it was built as more of a workhorse movement. That’s why it appeared in several non-chronometer models, including early Submariners, the later 5513, and the Air-King. This fine example features a beautiful gilt dial with a chapter ring, bold lume-filled numerals, and silver printing for "Explorer" and "Super Precision." The hands were replaced during a later service and are tritium—no longer glowing under UV light. The riveted Oyster bracelet (ref. 7206) has end links marked 80 and a clasp stamped 2/69. The watch has been fully checked and regulated by our watchmaker and is ready to “explore some more” in the years ahead! It wears beautifully on the wrist—classic and under-the-radar, never loud. Get in touch if this low-key legend speaks to you. Specifications: Rolex Explorer Reference 5504 Super precision 36mm case with smooth bezel Serial 571xxx dating to 1960 Black gilt chapter ring dial “Swiss only “with two silver lines Hand set later then the watch Riveted Oyster bracelet ref 7206 with end links 80 Fully checked by our watchmaker One-year mechanical warranty Free worldwide FedEx Priority shipping

title: Rolex Submariner "Purple Dial" Ref. 16618 in 18k Yellow Gold with Original Warranty Paper
description: We are thrilled to present an exceptional yellow gold Submariner with a remarkable twist! This reference 16618 boasts a stunning case and bezel, with sharp lines and a fascinating red oxidation detail on the case—adding a unique character to this iconic timepiece. The caseback features a French import stamp, further emphasizing the watch's historical and geographical provenance. The blue bezel insert remains unfaded and free of major imperfections, while the accompanying Oyster bracelet (Ref. 92908) is in excellent condition. It includes solid end links, a dive extension, and a clasp stamped “R 2.” The true standout feature is the dial, which has undergone a mesmerizing transformation from its original blue soleil finish to a rich purple hue. The tritium hour markers have aged beautifully to a warm beige tone, complementing the hands, which are slightly lighter but retain their tritium luminescence. Adding to its charm, this watch comes with its original punched warranty paper. Although the first letter of the serial number wasn’t punched and was later added by hand (as shown in the images), the warranty documents a sale in Paris in 1994—a delightful detail that aligns perfectly with the French import stamp on the caseback. Rolex’s ability to reimagine the rugged Submariner as a luxurious statement piece is truly remarkable, and this gold version perfectly balances tool watch functionality with the elegance of the jet-set lifestyle. If you love this exceptional piece as much as we do, we’d love to hear from you!
